data.title: Hermes: The Last Standing Ultra Luxury Stock
data.description:
Hermès (OTCPK:HESAF) (OTCPK:HESAY) shares have uncharacteristically lagged the market and many of its peers so far in 2025, with the stock down nearly 5% YTD.

The valuation bug seems to have finally hit Hermès, as investors don't find a reason to get too excited amid "business as usual" results and look for adventures elsewhere in the luxury sector.

I argue that with Hermès being the last-standing premium valued ultra luxury company amid struggles at Ferrari (RACE) and Brunello Cucinelli (OTCPK:BCUCF), investors should stay patient before re-entering the stock.

Tough Year for Ultra Luxury

I have written extensively about the luxury pyramid in my articles covering the sector. From Hermès and Ferrari to Kering (OTCPK:PPRUF), Richemont (OTCPK:CFRHF), and LVMH (OTCPK:LVMHF), it has become a common practice to classify luxury companies in different tiers based on their "luxuriousness."

In other words, the harder to get, the more expensive, and the more scarce, the higher you go in the pyramid.

In the stock market, you don't have to be a fashion expert to identify which company belongs where; all you have to do is look at the different earnings multiples in early 2025.

The rationale is simple. Companies at the top of the luxury pyramid cater to ultra-high-net-worth individuals, who aren't sensitive to macro swings, and maintain a demand-supply imbalance to drive scarcity. As a result, the market views them as companies with unparalleled resiliency and predictability. You could argue that the main limitation for these companies is the lack of greed of their managers (though they are doing so to protect their brands).

Meanwhile, companies at the lower tiers, like LVMH and Kering, generate a significant part of their sales from aspirational customers and operate with no demand-supply imbalance in the majority of their SKUs. For instance, during the COVID-19 peak, many people who were financially stable but certainly not rich bought LV handbags or Gucci shoes, and with the macro deteriorating, they are no longer allowing themselves to do so. As a result, these companies are far more sensitive to macro and are less predictable.

Because of the mechanics we discussed above, ultra-luxury stocks are more appealing during macro downtrends, while aspirational stocks tend to outperform in strong environments.

As we can see, over the past six months, the market has clearly been saying that this is the aspirational tier's time to shine. Ferrari had a disappointing investor day, and Brunello is dealing with a short report over its Russia business. Meanwhile, the market wants to get ahead of a possible recovery, creating an aspirational luxury bubble.

All of a sudden, the valuation gap is not that obvious, except for Hermès. As such, the bar for Hermès to provide upside to shareholders is the highest it's ever been. I maintain my "Hold" rating, which I previously discussed in August.

Third-Quarter Results Show Hermès Remains at the Top, but Gap is Narrowing

Hermès delivered revenue growth of 4.8% in the quarter, or 9.6% at constant exchange rates. Sales reached €3.9 billion, with leather goods leading the way, followed by other Hermès sectors, then other products.

On a geographical basis, all regions showed positive results, with every segment but Asia Pacific growing in double digits at constant rates. The Americas led the way with 14.1%, then Japan at 13.8%, then the Middle East with 11.7%. It's noteworthy that the Asia Pacific is slowly becoming a smaller portion of sales.

Similar to peers, Hermès delivered an acceleration in Q3. However, it was less significant than others. To Hermès' defense, it isn't lapping easy comps because, unlike LVMH and Kering, Hermès was growing at a mid-teens rate in 2024. Also, it's still operating at peak margins, whereas they are far lower.

Still, the narrowing of the gap represents a significant shift in trend.

For Hermès, this was a business-as-usual quarter, and at the current valuation, investors need more than that.

Valuation and Outlook

At €2,195 per share and $2,580 per HESAF ADR, Hermès trades at 52 times forward earnings and 45 times next year's estimates, which include 9% revenue growth and 15% EPS growth.

These valuation levels are right in the valuation range for Hermès. By and large, when a stock trades near the high end of its usual valuation range, something unusual needs to happen for it to deliver meaningful upside.

Meanwhile, continuing our discussion in the first section, the gap between Hermès and its ultra-luxury peers, in terms of valuation, is the highest it's been in years.

So, Hermès is being "attacked" from both sides. On the results front, the gap from aspirational names is narrowing. On the valuation front, the gap from alternative ultra-luxury names is expanding.

All in all, it would be a huge challenge for Hermès to deliver results that support market-beating performance, even though they are highly likely to continue delivering strong ~10% growth next quarter, in line with management's confidence on the call.

For me, Hermès would need to go down to about €1,960 per share, which would be 40 times next year's earnings, to become attractively valued.

Conclusion

Hermès is in a league of its own; there's no question about that. However, that's not enough for the stock to deliver market-beating performance.

At 52 times forward earnings, Hermès is the last standing ultra luxury stock with a multiple above 45x, making its valuation less appealing relatively speaking.

From another front, Hermès' struggling peers have been showing signs of improvement and narrowing the gap, also hurting the case for its significant premium.

Hermès remains an amazing company with an improbable path for market-beating returns, mandating a reiteration of the "Hold" rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: WTI Crude Surges Above $61 As U.S. Sanctions On Russia Ignite Supply Fears
data.description:
​By Jainam Mehta

WTI crude oil climbed back above $61 per barrel on Friday, marking its strongest weekly advance since early June. The rally follows sweeping new U.S. sanctions targeting Russia’s top oil producers, Rosneft and Lukoil, which together account for nearly half of the nation’s exports.

With China’s state-run refiners reportedly pausing seaborne Russian purchases and India preparing to reduce imports, global traders are bracing for a potential disruption that could tighten supply through the winter.

Geopolitics reignite oil market volatility

The latest sanctions mark one of Washington’s most forceful actions against Moscow since the conflict in Ukraine began, directly targeting about 2.2 million barrels per day of Russian oil exports. The European Union also moved to restrict Russian LNG imports and infrastructure investment, while Ukraine intensified strikes on refineries and export terminals. Together, these developments have revived supply-side risk in a market that had been weighed down by global demand concerns for much of the year.

President Donald Trump said he plans to discuss energy trade with Chinese President Xi Jinping, while praising India’s decision to begin cutting Russian oil purchases. The coordinated pressure on Moscow comes as the United States expands its own energy exports under the “energy dominance” strategy, adding another layer of complexity to global trade flows.

Still, traders are wary of overcommitting to the upside. Demand from Asia remains uneven, and refinery margins have narrowed in recent weeks. The result is a market torn between tightening supply and fragile consumption, with both forces dictating the next directional move.

Technical picture: Rebound faces decisive resistance

Technically, WTI’s recovery has brought prices into a pivotal range. On the daily chart, crude rebounded more than 10% from its recent low at $55, finding strong support at a level that has repeatedly held through past corrections. Immediate resistance now lies near $63.30, where the 50-day exponential moving average (EMA) meets the descending trendline from July highs.

A sustained breakout above $63–$65 would suggest a structural reversal, potentially paving the way for a rally toward $70. However, the broader setup still reflects a bearish undertone, with lower highs forming consistently since the summer peak near $80. A rejection at current levels could see prices roll back toward $58, while a decisive break below $55.20 would expose the next downside target near $52.

Momentum indicators show renewed strength but remain short of confirming a full trend shift. The rebound’s pace suggests speculative buying tied to the sanctions narrative rather than fundamental demand improvement, making the move particularly sensitive to headline risk.

Outlook: Bulls-eye breakout as risks stay balanced

As previously discussed, WTI crude’s rebound has turned sentiment cautiously optimistic, but the recovery remains fragile. If prices hold above $61 and breach the $63–$65 band, traders may view the rally as confirmation of a near-term reversal, with $70 emerging as the next key target. Conversely, a failure to sustain momentum could pull the market back into its longer-term downtrend, particularly if demand data softens or sanctions prove less disruptive than anticipated.

In the weeks ahead, attention will center on whether geopolitical disruptions translate into actual supply losses and how OPEC+ responds to the renewed volatility. For now, crude remains driven by geopolitics as much as by fundamentals—a balance that keeps traders on edge heading into November.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: Sonic Automotive: Q3 Performance Had Mixed Takeaways (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
My view of Sonic Automotive, Inc. (NYSE:SAH) has become "Neutral."

The Powersports business has a long growth runway. But its near-term profits will be under pressure due to medical cost inflation.

I was bullish on SAH earlier, considering its generous cash distributions and above-consensus 1Q24 numbers outlined in my April 29, 2024, article.

Cost Surge And Sales Composition Changes Led To Negative EPS Surprise

On Thursday, the firm issued a press release revealing its 3Q25 financials. Investors were disappointed with its latest disclosures, as evidenced by the 16% share price drop on the same day.

SAH's normalized bottom-line expansion moderated from 2Q25's +49.0% to +11.9% for the recent three-month period on a year-on-year basis. It registered an earnings of $1.41/share in Jul-Sep '25. That turned out to be 19% below the sell-side's forecast.

The group's performance was hurt by "gross profit" or "GP" weakness and higher expenses in certain categories.

The number of "electric vehicles" or "EVs" sold by its core dealership business was up +54% sequentially in the third quarter this year. That was attributable to pull-forward demand driven by the "federal tax credit expiry" at the end of September.

SAH revealed at the analyst briefing that the EV "mix (within the dealership segment) went from 8.3% in Q2 to 11.9% in Q3." The enterprise's new vehicle GP/unit took a $300 hit as a result and decreased 7% YoY to $2,852.

Its year-on-year "Finance & Insurance" gross profit per unit growth also slowed from +14% in 2Q2025 to 3Q2025's +7% for the same reason. Sonic's call commentary drew attention to this quarter's "warranty penetration" decline brought about by "higher lease mix on BEV (Battery Electric Vehicle)."

As per S&P Capital IQ data, SAH's EBITDA-to-sales contracted by 0.56 ppts (percentage points) YoY for the 3rd quarter of '25. This represented a 101bps (basis points) miss. An unfavorable change in revenue structure biased towards EVs was the key issue.

Separately, the increase in healthcare/insurance- and taxation-related costs was a drag on its profitability. Its 3Q2025 normalized earnings per share were 36% below what it achieved in the preceding quarter.

At the results meeting, Sonic highlighted that it is "self-insured" and negatively affected by "an increase in medical premiums."

Management estimated that SAH's 3Q25 earnings were $0.05/share and $0.10/share lower on QoQ and YoY terms, respectively, due to this headwind.

Its latest quarterly effective tax rate was 34%, or much higher than 22% a year ago. There wasn't an explanation provided for the sharp rise.

Moving forward, both EVs and taxation will be less of a concern, but medical expenses remain a problem.

As mentioned above, the EV volume spike was a one-off, as consumers brought forward their purchases to take advantage of incentives. Also, SAH has guided for a 4Q25 tax-to-income ratio of 30.25%, which is milder than 3Q2025's mid-30s figure.

Sonic is anticipating a low-70s percent SG&A-to-GP in the final three months of '25. This is comparable to the actual Q3 metric of 72.8%.

Healthcare-related inflation is an industry-wide challenge that SAH can't avoid. SA News reported last month that "employer-sponsored healthcare plans in the U.S. are set to face the biggest cost hike in 15 years next year" due to "rising healthcare prices and utilization."

But Revenue Beat Bodes Well For Mid-Term Outlook

It wasn't all doom and gloom for Sonic.

SAH's 3Q25 turnover went up 13.8% year-on-year to $4.0 billion. This was a 10% outperformance versus consensus.

The company grew slower at +5.9% in 2Q2025. It even suffered from four consecutive quarters of top-line contractions in the 4Q23-3Q24 time frame.

Bright spots for SAH were the "Powersports" business's potential and the growing exposure to the high-margin luxury category.

Sonic's Powersports-related sales and GP grew 42% and 32% to $84 million and $23 million, respectively, in the third quarter of the current year. These numbers were equivalent to new historical highs. SAH credited this to the sale of "1,105 million new and used motorcycles or Harley's" at the "85th Sturgis Motorcycle Rally." The Sturgis event is organized by a subsidiary that it took over in '23.

Average M&A Valuations For Various Dealership Types

The above-mentioned chart offers an excellent illustration of inorganic growth opportunities for the Powersports arm. It contributed a mere 1% of SAH's FY24 revenue.

Powersports-focused dealers are changing hands at low-to-mid single-digit multiples of pre-tax profit. It's reasonable for me to think that Sonic can be an industry consolidator over time by buying small and privately held players on the cheap.

Also, SAH's earnings release touched on "the acquisition of our latest Jaguar Land Rover (JLR) store in Santa Monica." The firm emphasized that the move "cements Sonic as the largest Jaguar Land Rover volume retailer in the U.S. and enhances our brand portfolio in the coveted California luxury market."

I believe this development will be margin-accretive for the firm in the future.

At its Q3 briefing, SAH indicated that JLR has the "highest GPU (Gross Profit/Unit) stores in the company." It also commented that "we've benefited from scale by being the largest dealer now for JLR."

I infer from management's comments that the enlarged luxury presence will most likely translate into superior gross margins and beneficial fixed-cost leverage effects.

It's inevitable that luxury-related automobiles are less popular in times of macroeconomic weakness. Looking beyond the near term, expanding the luxury dealership should eventually lead to profitability enhancement across a full economic cycle.

My Concluding Remarks

I've cut SAH's rating from "Buy" to "Hold."

It's highly probable that SG&A costs will remain elevated in the short term. I'm projecting a further EPS growth deceleration to the high-single-digit percent level in 4Q25.

But its prospects for the long run are promising, considering Powersports' growth and the scaling up of the luxury sub-segment. My forecasts point to its revenue CAGR going from FY19-24's +6.4% to FY2025-2029's +8%.

SAH is reasonably valued at a next twelve months' P/E of 9.2 times.

The mean forward EPS multiple for its peers, Asbury Automotive Group, Inc. (ABG), AutoNation, Inc. (AN), Group 1 Automotive, Inc. (GPI), Lithia Motors, Inc. (LAD), and Penske Automotive Group, Inc. (PAG) is 10.0x, or just 9% above Sonic.

data.title: LCNB Corp.: A 'Buy' On Strong Results And Robust Dividend Yield
data.description:
Introduction

It has been several years since I last discussed LCNB Corp. (NASDAQ:LCNB). The bank recently reported strong results for the third quarter of this year, in combination with a low amount of non-performing loans. It is now a good time to have another look at this regional bank, active in Ohio.

A Strong Earnings Result In The Third Quarter

Looking at the bank's financial results in the third quarter of this year, we see it reported a total interest income of $26.3 million, relatively stable compared to the third quarter of 2024. Meanwhile, the total amount of interest expenses decreased rather substantially, and this allowed the bank to report a 20% increase in the net interest income.

As LCNB also reported a net non-interest expense of approximately $9.4 million, the pre-tax and pre-provision income was approximately $8.7 million. And as you can see in the image above, LCNB recorded a total loan loss provision of $0.2 million, which is just one-third of the amount of provision it recorded in the same quarter a year ago.

This ultimately resulted in a net income of $6.9 million, which works out to $0.49 per share. That's approximately 60% higher than in the third quarter of 2024 thanks to the increased net interest income and lower loan loss provisions, partially offset by a slightly higher net non-interest expense.

The strong result in the third quarter is also substantially better than the performance in the first half of the year. Although loan loss provisions remained limited in the first semester, the acceleration of the net interest income plays a major role in the bank’s bottom line result.

LCNB currently pays a quarterly dividend of $0.22 per share, and it goes without saying that the recent results confirm the dividend is very well covered. The payout ratio based on the earnings in the third quarter was approximately 45%, and looking at the payout ratio for the first nine months of the current financial year, we're looking at an approximate payout ratio of around 50%.

Another key element to keep in mind is the bank’s balance sheet. I, of course, want to keep an eye on the quality of its loan book. As you can see below, the bank had a total asset base of just over $2.2 billion, of which $36 million was held in cash, with an additional $71 million in securities (mainly debt securities), with the majority consisting of securities available for sale that are marked to market.

I am very interested in seeing the quality of the loan book, which stood at $1.67 billion at the end of September. Although the bank hasn't filed its detailed quarterly financials just yet (which will contain more data on the loan book), LCNB was able to confirm that as of the end of the third quarter, less than $2 million of the loans were classified as non-performing.

This represents approximately 0.12% of the total amount of loans, and it is a substantial improvement from the 0.28% in the past few quarters. And with just $2 million in loans that are no longer performing versus a $12 million accumulated loan loss provision, LCNB seems to have a good grip on its loan book situation, and that bodes well for the future. I also don't expect to see a major difference in the breakdown of the loan book, which, as you can see below, predominantly consisted of commercial real estate at the end of Q2.

In any case, the recent results of LCNB indicate having exposure to commercial real estate is not necessarily a bad thing as long as you focus on quality. The robust earnings as well as the low amount of non-performing loans are very encouraging to see.

Investment Thesis

At the end of the third quarter, the bank had almost $270 million in shareholder's equity, and after deducting the approximately $100 million in goodwill and other intangibles, the tangible book value was approximately $170 million. Divided over the current share count of 14.2 million shares, this results in a tangible book value of approximately $12/share.

While this means the bank is still trading at a premium of approximately 25% to its tangible book value per share, the gap is closing pretty fast given the relatively low dividend payments versus the reported earnings. I expect the bank to end 2026 with a tangible book value exceeding $13 per share, and given the strong earnings result and the low amount of non-performing loans, I am more than happy to pay the current premium.

Let's also not forget that despite the relatively low payout ratio, the dividend yield is currently approximately 5.9%, which I think offers an excellent compensation while waiting for the share price to increase. I currently have no position in LCNB, but this could be an opportunity to initiate a long position. I maintain my "Buy" rating.

data.title: Realty Income FQ3 Earnings Preview: Best Time To Buy Since COVID
data.description:
O's current relative discount is larger than in 2020

I last covered Realty Income (NYSE:O) stock on 9.11 with an article titled "Realty Income: 2025 Investment Volume Foreshadows AFFO And Dividend Growth". It mainly focused on my forward projection of its investment activities and concluded with a buy rating. Since then, a few new developments have emerged both in terms of macroeconomics, the overall REIT sector, and also some business specific changes for O. In the rest of this follow-up article, I will elaborate on the top 3 catalysts on my list: the stock's dividend yield spread relative to the REIT sector, the changes in interest outlook, and also its upcoming FQ3 earnings report (ER) scheduled on Nov 3, 2025. I will explain why these developments have led me to conclude that the current conditions offer an entry point as compelling as that during the COVID panic sell for O shares.

Let me start with the first catalyst. Since my last writing, both O and the REIT sector fund VNQ have declared their latest dividend payouts. These declarations, when combined with the price changes, have led to valuation discount of O shares wider than that seen during the COVID panic sale judging by the dividend yield spread (YS) shown below. To wit, this chart shows the YS of O relative to VNQ, which is used as a proxy for the REIT sector as a whole, in the past 10 years. As seen, the YS has widened from the time of my last writing to the current level of 1.489%. The current YS is thus not far above the 10-year average of 0.498% but also noticeably above the level observed amid the COVID correction (of around 1.2%), suggesting O's unusually attractive valuation relative to the overall REIT sector.

Of course, wider YS, cheap valuation, and/or higher yield does not automatically make a good investment. We will have to make sure the business fundamentals remain sound, which is the case in my view as to be explained next.

O stock: FQ3 earnings projection implies safer payout

Given the central role of dividends in O's investment thesis, let me start with its dividend safety. As aforementioned, O is scheduled to announce its next earnings on Nov 3, 2025, for its FQ3. According to consensus estimate shown below, its normalized FFO is expected to reach $1.07 per share. Also, a just mentioned, the company has recently declared its latest monthly dividend payout of $0.2695 per share with an ex-dividend date of Oct 31. With these changes, the FWD FFO dividend payout ratio for FQ3 2025 is ~75.7%. In comparison, the payout ratio for the previous quarter is about 76.4% based on a normalized FFO of $1.06 per share.

Expanding the horizon a bit more, the FWD payout ratio is not only lower by sequential comparison but also below the company's long-term averages as you can see from the following chart. More specifically, O's cash payout ratio has averaged around 80.63% in the past 10 years and peaked around 90% in the aftermath of the COVID pandemic. The current below-average payout ratio, combined with my outlook for interest rates to be detailed next, supports more potential for continued or even accelerated dividend growth going forward.

O stock: leverage

Potential investors should certainly monitor the company's financial strength closely with its rising debts lately. As a background, O's total debt has experienced some noticeable expansions in recent quarters. For example, it increased by 11.4% in the second quarter, from the previous year's level, to $28.35 billion. These increases were mostly driven by additions to its property portfolio (the focus of my last article). A notable recent example was the $9.3 billion acquisition of retail and industrial REIT Spirit Realty Capital. The debt increase, when combined with rising costs and interest expense (which increased more than 13% in the past quarter), could certainly pressure bottom-line growth in the near term.

However, my own overall assessment is that its balance sheet remains sound. Admittedly, the debt-to-total capital ratio has been on an increasing trend lately since its bottom in 2022 as seen in the chart below. However, the current level of 43.75% is still quite manageable in my view. It is still below long-term average of 44.04% and also considerably below the peak levels of around 49% observed right before the COVID correction.

Furthermore, I expect the pressure of borrowing costs to gradually ease in the years ahead given the updated interest rate outlook as detailed next.

Other risks and final thoughts

Another material development since my last writing is the updated dot-plot provided by the Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) as shown below. According to this dot-plot, the median of the expected interest rate range shows a gradual decline in the next few years. To wit, the median is expected to be reduced to a range between 3.50% to 3.75% by 2025, which will remain for 2026. Further cuts are expected to resume in 2027 to reduce the median to a range between 3.0% to 3.25% by the end of 2027. These cuts, if/when they materialize, could enhance O's capital allocation flexibility for the years to come.

I open the article with an assertion that O is trading at a wide valuation discount relative to VNQ in terms of YS. However, investors should be aware that other valuation metrics could paint a different picture. For example, in terms of P/AFFO ratio, the current reading of 14.3x (on a TTM basis) suggests close-to-fair valuation relative to the sector. In terms of P/FFO ratios, O's current multiples suggest a mild degree of overvaluation.

As a vertical comparison, the last chart compares O's dividend current yield relative to its own historical ranges. I trust dividends as a more reliable profit metric (say than FFO or AFFO) given O's strong dividend track. If you subscribe to this view too, than you can see that its current yield (of around 5.4% on an FWD basis based on its latest declaration) suggests valuation almost as attractive as that observed during the peak of the COVID panic sale.

To close, this reexamination on Realty mainly focused on three new catalysts: its latest dividend yield compared to VNQ, the updated interest outlook, and also its upcoming FQ3 ER. These changes have led me to view the current conditions as an equally compelling- if not more compelling - entry point as that during the COVID panic sell for potential O investors. The current valuation discounts, either compared to VNQ or its own historical norm, are comparable to those observed at the peak of the panic sell. Yet the fundamentals are stronger in my model based on my projections of its dividend coverage, leverage ratios, and also borrowing costs.

data.title: Nextdoor: Still A Prove-It Story
data.description:
Nextdoor Holdings, Inc. (NYSE:NXDR) has been quite volatile since my last sell rating. It was down roughly 13% at one point, then up about 18%, and is now trading near the same level where I last left it. Each swing reflects the market’s ongoing tug-of-war between structural flaws in the company’s business model and the bursts of optimism surrounding its new initiatives. I still think investors should approach this one with caution. You might miss some upside if a turnaround takes hold, but that’s a fair trade-off for avoiding a business that hasn’t yet proved it can stand on solid ground. There is still a gap between user participation and advertiser relevance. Until that changes, it’s a “prove-it” story in my book.

Management Commentary at the Goldman Sachs Conference

The company presented at the Goldman Sachs conference in September and the tone at the conference seemed a tad cautious to me. The below quote sums it up and I’d say it also sets up the stock for a bit of volatility before results from new initiatives kick in.

But -- and I know that this is a very frustrating thing for investors today to hear and it's frustrating for me to say. But I'd say we're still early. We're still early. The reason we're early is not because, we don't know what we're doing or because we haven't found signal, it's because we want to be extremely careful this time around that we don't take shortcuts.

Additionally, on monetization there’s one thing I liked and another that seemed like the same old thing again. The thing I liked was the focus on growing durable revenue. The pathway to that durable revenue is unclear as durable revenue ultimately requires durable value, but the shift in tone is at least theoretically encouraging. And the thing I disliked is describing engagement as a leverage which I think reveals a big blind spot. The blind spot is commercial intent. Engagement for its own sake doesn’t solve the monetization problem unless it produces content that advertisers value. If you look at the Nextdoor subreddit, which is a place for interesting Nextdoor posts, it's still quite an active subreddit. So, there hasn’t been much of a shift on that front.

Q3 Preview and Valuation

Because of the cautious tone in the conference, the expectations of inflection in the third quarter results are low. Consensus revenue estimates are roughly flat at $66.5 million compared to $65.6 million from the previous year’s quarter. In Q1, the company pivoted from total WAUs to platform WAUs, which is a more active base. I’d like to hear some commentary about an uptick in monetizable activity in this base as it would point to a healthier engagement. Conversely, continued stagnation would reinforce the argument that engagement remains largely non-commercial.

Operating leverage will also be in focus. The company still reported an adjusted EBITDA loss of $2 million in Q2, but this non-GAAP figure masks the true profitability. The difference in the net loss of $15 million and the adjusted EBITDA is almost entirely explained by the $17.1 million in stock-based compensation (SBC), which management adds back to calculate its adjusted figure.

With adjusted figures, I find that adding back recurring SBC expense does not represent the actual cash available to shareholders. And the company still has $104.4 million in unrecognized compensation that will be expensed over the next 1.9 years, acting as a significant, ongoing drag on future GAAP profitability.

Therefore, while management is guiding for quarterly adjusted EBITDA breakeven by Q4, investors should be aware that this target ignores a substantial, recurring expense and does not represent true, economic profitability.

For the comparable company analysis, I have selected a group of different platforms with ties to the hyperlocal economy. Yelp is a local business directory. Angi is a marketplace for local home services. Yext is a bit of an outlier in that its product is a B2B software. But its purpose is to help businesses manage their local digital presence and therefore has ties to the local economy.

Based on this peer group, only Yext appears to be significantly mispriced. The EV/Sales is probably a better metric as it accounts appropriately for the unique levels of cash and debt. For instance, Nextdoor’s higher P/S is due to its large net cash position. Except for Yelp, these companies are also not consistently profitable making a sales metric more appropriate at this point in time.

When we plot the EV/Sales against forward revenue growth there appears to be a rational relationship between Angi, Yelp, and Nextdoor. As the growth rate improves (from -6.97% to 4.48% to 8.21%), the EV/Sales multiple appropriately increases (from 0.73 to 1.25 to 1.61). The EV/Sales line from Angi to Yelp appears a bit more steep which one would expect as a premium paid for getting out of a decline.

Yext is the clear outlier here and appears overvalued as it trades at a higher multiple despite the expectations of a lower growth rate.

Conclusion

To me, the stock still fits into the category of optionality without visibility. The company has time, cash, and ambition, but not the evidence of sustainable economics. Volatility before results from new initiatives is likely and sentiment may oscillate around product updates. In general, to realize the maximum return, you need to see some evidence of a potential kickstart in growth. But the structural problems in low-value engagement make me look for a greater degree of certainty in the quality of evidence. So, I am still maintaining a bearish stance on Nextdoor with the mentality of a potential investor who might say “prove it before I invest”.

data.title: Boston Scientific: Continuing Its M&A Playbook, As Organic Achievements Impress
data.description:
At the start of 2024, I concluded that continued M&A efforts were driving growth in the case of Boston Scientific (NYSE:BSX) an observation that remains very true today.

At the time, shares hit fresh all-time highs at levels near the $60 mark, as M&A was accompanied by real mid- to high-single digit organic growth, impressive given the M&A strategy employed as well.

In the meantime, the company stuck to posting impressive organic growth rates, in fact, organic growth has only accelerated, while it continues to play its M&A playbook. In less than two years' time, revenues are up some 40%, which, combined with margin expansion, means that almost all the impressive share price gains have been backed up by real earnings growth.

This makes me comforted on the one hand, yet a 3% earnings yield feels a bit low in this interest rate environment, although the performance remains too impressive to disregard the shares here.

Shedding Some Perspective

In the decade leading up to 2024, Boston Scientific doubled sales from $7 billion to $14 billion, although the company incurred some 10% dilution over this period of time. M&A and organic growth grew the business larger in terms of size, but also more diversified across categories.

While this was to be applauded, I had not forgotten about the past, as a $27 billion deal for Guidant caused shares to rise to a high (at the time) around $40 per share. After that, the company saw its shares being pressured, with shares for the first time revisiting those levels in 2019.

Given that past episode and the fundamentals, I was leaning cautious. After all, the company was on track to post earnings around $2 per share for all of 2023, as a $58 stock was valued at a 29 times earnings multiple. With nearly 1.5 billion shares trading at $58 and factoring in $8 billion in net debt, the $93 billion enterprise valuation valued the business at 6.5 times sales of $14 billion, and at a 30 earnings multiple, both being rather demanding multiples.

All this was about to change as the business announced a $3.4 billion deal to acquire medical device company Axonics, adding $366 million in revenues for the treatment of overactive bladder and fecal incontinence. In all, I ended up concluding that Boston deserved more credit over time, yet that overall expectations were too high for me to get involved with the shares here.

Strong Momentum

In the near two years since the start of 2024, shares of Boston Scientific have seen gains of more than 60%, now trading at the $103 mark, down from an absolute high of $109 per share.

Forwarding to February of this year, Boston Scientific reported a near 18% increase in full year sales to $16.7 billion, almost all driven by organic growth. This was driven by a 22% organic increase in cardiovascular sales to $10.8 billion, with a near $6 billion MedSurg business posting sales up 10% on the year before. The strong momentum drove earnings growth in a major way as well, with adjusted earnings up from $2.05 per share in 2023 to $2.51 per share in 2024.

The company guided for a 10-12% increase in organic sales growth, with reported sales growth seen 2.5% higher, driving earnings per share, which were seen moving up towards $2.80-$2.87 per share.

Momentum was only accelerating as the company continued to pursue M&A this year. This includes the purchase of a remaining 74% stake in Bolt Medical at the start of the year. This, however, is another bolt-on deal, with the remaining shares valued at $443 million, excluding $221 million in regulatory milestones payable.

A similar-sized deal followed in March, when the company announced the purchase of a remaining 90% stake in SoniVie, with the price for the remaining shares pegged at $380 million, excluding a potential $180 million regulatory milestone payment due.

Aided by continued M&A efforts, the company reported a 21% increase in first quarter sales, again largely driven by organic growth. As a result, the company hiked the full year organic growth guidance by two points to 12-14%, with adjusted earnings seen between $2.87 and $2.94 per share.

Second quarter sales grew by nearly 23%, with revenues now comfortably trending at $20 billion per annum, as full year earnings are now seen at a midpoint of $2.97 per share.

Third quarter sales grew by another 20%, with organic growth still exceeding a healthy 15%. Continued momentum across the board means that the midpoint of the full year earnings guidance has been hiked further to $3.03 per share. The very strong organic performance has been driven by strong Cardiovascular results, certainly in the Watchman and Electrophysiology segments. With the share count coming in just shy of 1.5 billion shares, the market value of the firm has risen to $154 billion.

Net debt is reported at $11.0 billion as of the second quarter (with the third quarter 8-K filing not yet made), yet with adjusted EBITDA trending comfortably above $5 billion, leverage ratios are very reasonable at just over 2 times. Trading around the $103 mark, the $165 billion enterprise valuation pushed up the valuation to 8 times sales, and while margins have improved, earnings multiples have expanded to 34 times currently, however for good reasons, that of near continued >15% organic growth rates!

More M&A

In a typical deal for Boston Scientific, the latest acquisition was announced in October. The company reached a $533 million cash deal to acquire the remaining equity stake in Nalu Medical, after the company had been an investor in the business since 2017.

The deal is designed to expand its ability to develop and commercialize innovative and minimally invasive solutions for patients with chronic pain. With a $60 million revenue contribution, the deal is not cheap at 10 times sales (with 100% of the equity valued at $600 million), but so is Boston itself.

The deal is seen as boosting sales by 0.3%, adding to the growth profile, with 2026 growth rates seen in excess of 25%!

A Final Thought

The truth is that a 3% earnings yield normally does not look that enticing, certainly not in this interest rate environment. However, Boston Scientific is clearly setting itself apart from its peers, posting 20% growth rates. Moreover, the observation is that these are largely organic growth achievements, although M&A plays a role in this as well.

After all, bolt-on M&A efforts only count for the first year as inorganic growth, but when they are scaled up within Boston Scientific thereafter, their growth from year two counts as organic growth. Certainly there is an element of that, although those real organic growth achievements are very impressive as well.

In between all this, I am hard-pressed to chase the shares here, although that relative appeal seems on the rise here, with shares trading largely flattish so far in 2025. This makes me interested to watch continued M&A moves, with a real intent of buying even relatively modest dips here.

data.title: Evolution AB: I'm Not Giving Up Yet, But My Patience Is Running Out
data.description:
Yesterday, Evolution AB (publ) (OTCPK:EVVTY) released its Q3 2025 report and the market reaction was quite negative. The stock dropped 8%, and I was personally disappointed as well by this quarter. Net sales decreased 2.40%, cybersecurity remains a major issue in Asia, and after the European ring-fencing, the regulatory risk is now spreading towards all of Asia (India, Philippines above all).

This is getting frustrating, as the improvements we saw in Q2 2025 have been totally wiped out. CEO Martin Carlesund is confident about a recovery, but he didn’t provide any guidance, so we have no clue how long these problems will continue.

Let’s see in detail what this Q3 2025 is all about.

A big problem to solve

The situation in Asia is getting serious, and this is affecting the whole company: this is the root of almost all problems.

Asia accounts for 37% of total revenues and this makes it the most important geographical region. In Q3 2025, revenues declined 6.50% YoY and 9.60% QoQ, a very disappointing performance.

During the last quarter we saw that revenues started growing once again, so investors (including me) were optimistic about a potential recovery; but now all the progress has been wiped out. Cybersecurity concerns are still there, and on top of this, new regulatory problems arose.

So the situation on the market in Asia, is a lot of different countries. We point out, Philippines is very volatile right now. There's things happening in India, but there's also other countries that are fluctuating and things are happening there as well. India is a large country. There are regions that have portions regulated when it comes to sports. There's a desire in some regions to regulate. Now there are suggestions on a sort of federal level to take a few steps towards blocking online gaming. These types of movements we often see, when there is talks and happenings about regulation, it opens up for different routes forward. It could go into regulation, it could settle down or it could go to somewhere else. We can't speculate on that. We just see that it's affecting us to a certain level right now.

CEO Martin Carlesund.

India and the Philippines are going through a period of new regulations toward online gaming, and we can’t know how all this will evolve. The management didn’t elaborate further on the matter, they only specified that India and the Philippines are not the only regions involved in this regulatory process. This definitely doesn’t bode well.

About cybersecurity issues, this theme has been going on for several quarters and now it is getting frustrating. The Q2 2025 results offered a glimmer of hope, but this Q3 2025 shows an entirely different picture.

I understand that it causes some frustration, especially since we actually saw some signs of improvement in the second quarter. And believe me, I'm frustrated, too. I can only repeat that it requires patience while being a top priority.

CEO Martin Carlesund.

Even Martin Carlesund seems quite nervous about it, and as a shareholder, I am getting worried about this cybersecurity issue. At first, I thought it was a momentary problem, and the Q2 2025 proved that I was right, but now my view is changing. What if this problem is permanent in Asia?

During the conference call, management stated that what is happening in Asia is something that can’t be solved with money. Evolution is a cash machine with no debt, so it could afford the best cybersecurity products on the market, but this seems not to be enough. Management urges patience, but mine is gradually running out. I would like to see more details on how the company is managing this issue as this reticence is not helping. The only thing that we do know is that there won’t be any improvement over the next quarter.

When it comes to Asia, to address this problem, it's technically difficult. It's very advanced, and we're doing it. And we're tuning and we're finding. My belief is over time, that we will find the right balance and the right solutions and continuously enhance and protect our product to make it even better. So in the longer perspective, I look at a very good situation in the Asian market. Now I don't have any -- I can't share any sort of time frame on that right now.

CEO Martin Carlesund.

As time goes by, I am getting less optimistic about Asia as a whole. It represents 37% of total revenue (last year 39%), but at this rate it will become a minor segment; this shrinking process will necessarily hurt the overall revenue growth of the next years. However, out of this mess we can still find some positive news:

The first one is that Evolution is improving its entire cybersecurity system. The investments made in Asia are useful to fight the same problems all over the world. To some extent, this further improves Evolution’s competitive advantage.

Second, since Asia is the region where Evolution gets the most of its unregulated revenue, the company is improving its exposure to regulated revenue. The latter are now 46% of the total, a year ago just 39%.

Overall, the situation in Asia remains very challenging, and I wonder whether this issue is momentary or not. Some months ago, I was sure about the first one.

Why I am still optimistic

At this point you might think that my strong buy rating makes no sense, after all the most important region where revenue comes from is declining by double digits. Well, we shouldn’t overlook what is happening in Asia, but Evolution is not just that.

North America and Latin America both experienced an expansion: revenue grew 14.50% and 6.40% YoY, respectively. The company is planning to open a second studio in Michigan by mid-2026, and now Crazy Time (one of the most successful live-casino games) is available in Connecticut. About the Galaxy Gaming acquisition, the management seems quite certain to get the regulatory approval by year-end. Regarding Latin America, the new studio in São Paulo is performing as expected and it will probably be expanded.

Let’s now turn to Europe, a region that is pretty close to Asia in terms of revenue.

Europe has the potential to become the most important region over the next quarters as regulatory risk seems behind us. The QoQ revenue growth is little but gives rise to hope that the bottom was reached last quarter.

We saw the full effect of our protective ring-fencing measures in the second quarter, and this provided a new foundation for growth. Worth mentioning is also that we got recognition for our ring fencing from one of the largest regulators in Europe, where we were pointed out as one of the best B2B suppliers. I'm happy with the progress in Europe in this quarter.

CEO Martin Carlesund.

As you can see, if we exclude Asia from the equation, the picture is not that bad. Even profitability still looks impressive.

The net profit margin is 50%, something that only a few companies in the world can achieve. Of course, revenue growth is disappointing, but I think it is worth waiting a few more quarters before throwing in the towel. If the profitability remains so high, it means that this company still has its competitive advantage, and the underlying business is extremely interesting.

To make you understand how undervalued this company potentially is, I want to make an absurd assumption: what if Evolution doesn’t grow over the next 10 years? Would it still be worth it?

Well, it could still deliver a 12% annual return over the next 10 years. This shows how much negativity currently surrounds this company. If it gets back to growing double digits (10%), the 10Y CAGR on your investment spikes to 20%, doubling the long-term stock market average return. I wouldn’t rule out this possibility, that’s why I didn’t sell a single share.

Consider that I am not factoring in any share buybacks, even though the company is committed to doing so.

In Q3 2025, there were share repurchases of €187 million; €406.50 million YTD. Instead of issuing the large dividend (4.40% yield), I would appreciate a further focus on buyback, but this probably won’t happen.

Conclusion

Evolution is facing a very challenging phase where its most important segment doesn’t grow anymore. We still don’t see the light at the end of the tunnel, but at least Europe and America are showing encouraging signs.

As stated by the CEO Martin Carlesund, more patience is needed to see a full recovery, but it probably won’t be in Q4 2025. I would like more details on the matter, but I remain optimistic thanks to the higher profitability and the DCF valuation.

The market's vision is blurred by negativity, but we know that this is the moment where you get a bargain. Of course, investing in Evolution provides lots of risks as we have seen, but I want to give it more time. I won’t buy more shares just because I already invested too much in it: diversification matters.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: U.S. Sanctions Rattle The Oil Market
data.description:
By Warren Patterson, Head of Commodities Strategy

The US sanctions two key Russian oil producers

Oil prices received a jolt higher this week after the Trump administration announced sanctions on Russian oil producers, Rosneft and Lukoil. These are the first direct sanctions that the administration has placed on Russia during President Trump’s second term. The announcement comes after plans for a summit between President Trump and President Putin in Budapest fell through, leaving Trump increasingly frustrated at the lack of progress in Russia and Ukraine moving towards a peace deal.

Rosneft and Lukoil produce more than a combined 5m b/d of crude oil, which is in the region of 50% of total Russian oil production and so these sanctions have the potential to be very disruptive to the oil market.

These are not the first Russian oil producers to be sanctioned by the US. In January, ahead of President Biden leaving the White House, sanctions were placed on Gazprom Neft and Surgutneftegas, along with a large portion of Russia’s shadow tanker fleet. These sanctions also led to initial concerns over supply disruptions; however, they ultimately had little impact on Russian oil flows.

These latest sanctions are set to come into full effect on 21 November 2025, allowing a wind-down of transactions with both Rosneft and Lukoil, as well as with any subsidiaries in which they hold 50% or more ownership.

The risk of buyers being penalised for transacting with Rosneft and Lukoil is a concern for the market and threatens a large portion of Russian oil supply.

Why has the Trump administration taken this action now?

Apart from President Trump growing increasingly frustrated with Putin and the lack of progress toward a peace deal, the state of the oil market also provides the US with the opportunity to take a more aggressive stance against Russia. Prior to the announcement of sanctions, the market was set for a significant surplus through the remainder of this year and 2026, which has weighed heavily on oil prices, with Brent trading down towards US$60/bbl. Lower oil prices create the opportunity to impose sanctions, whilst expectations of abundant supply also provide comfort that the market can manage potential losses in Russian oil supply.

What does this mean for Russian oil flows?

For the oil market, the most important point is how impactful these sanctions will be on Russian oil flows. This includes both crude oil and refined products. Russia exports a little more than 4.5m b/d of crude oil and in the region of 2.5m b/d of refined products, of which around 1m b/d is diesel. This helps explain the strength seen in the ICE gasoil crack over the week, amid concerns of further tightness in the middle distillate market.

Looking at the crude oil market, there are clear signs that buyers are looking for alternative grades, particularly from the Middle East. This is evident when looking at the Brent-Dubai spread, which has collapsed since the sanctions' announcement (driven by strength in the Dubai benchmark).

China is the largest buyer of Russian crude oil, taking 2m b/d so far this year. This will include both volumes via pipeline as well as by vessel. Meanwhile, flows to India have averaged a little more than 1.5m b/d so far this year. Other buyers include Turkey, which has taken roughly 290k b/d so far this year, while Hungary and Slovakia, which received an exemption from the EU ban on Russian oil, source almost all their needs from Russia via the Druzhba pipeline.

For the oil market, most focus will be on the sizeable volumes going into China and India. For India, there are already reports that Indian refiners are looking for alternative grades from the Middle East. Prior to these sanctions, India was facing secondary tariffs due to its purchases of Russian oil. Therefore, there had been talk already that Russian flows to India would fall dramatically.

For China, there have also been reports that state refiners there have backed out of some oil purchases following the sanction announcement. However, we believe that Russian oil flows to the country will likely remain more resilient. In fact, there is potential for volumes to pick up, particularly if India stops buying, which could see China picking up Urals at an even larger discount.

We could also see smaller buyers, such as Hungary, Slovakia and Turkey, seek alternative supply. However, it would not be surprising if Hungary and Slovakia seek an exemption from the US, citing difficulties in obtaining alternative supplies, as both countries are landlocked.

There will be an element of scepticism over how effective these sanctions will be. Russia has demonstrated its ability to keep oil flows going despite sanctions and embargoes since 2022. The price action that we have seen since the announcement appears to reflect these doubts, with Brent up a little more than 5% on the day sanctions were issued. Clearly, one would expect much more of a move if the belief was that a meaningful amount of Russian supply would be lost.

Does this dramatically shift the outlook for the oil market?

There is still plenty of uncertainty over how much Russian oil supply will be disrupted by these latest sanctions, and as a result, there is significant uncertainty about what this means for the oil balance and prices.

Prior to these sanctions, we held a bearish view on the market, expecting Brent to average $57/bbl over 2026, with prices weighed down by the scale of the surplus set to hit the market. We are holding back from making any revisions to our forecasts until there is more clarity on exactly what the impact of these sanctions will be on oil flows.

However, the shift seen from the Trump administration and the risk of further sanctions suggest that the floor for the market could end up being somewhat higher.

In addition, if we start seeing Russian flows becoming increasingly disrupted, we could see a scenario where Brent trades up to the $70-75/bbl range, assuming we lose in the region of 1.5m b/d of Russian supply (essentially all supply to India).

Much will also depend on how other OPEC+ members respond. The group has brought a significant amount of supply back onto the market since April this year, and any supply losses from Russia leave room for the group to tap further into its spare production capacity, helping to cap the upside in prices.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: Intel: Momentum Keeps On Building Based On The Turnaround (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Intel Corporation (NASDAQ:INTC) has been one of the most closely followed earnings releases by investors following the slew of investments made in the company. This has pushed the company’s stock up by more than 90%, without accounting for the current pre-market rise. Following my last analysis on the company back in September, I want to see how the story might have changed with the recent earnings.

To remind readers, Intel has been undergoing a major shift since Lip-Bu Tan took over as CEO, focusing on profitability and key partnerships to help the company go back to the forefront of innovation, mainly in its AI strategy.

Current Dynamics

Intel just announced a strong double beat, further fueling its upward momentum. Q3 Non-GAAP EPS came in at 23 cents, a beat of 22 cents, while revenue reached $13.7B, up 3.2% Y/Y and a beat of over $560MM. What a difference a year makes, especially when looking at net income that came in at around $4.1B, while a year prior, it was a loss of more than $16.6B. I was particularly interested in the performance of its Client Computing Group, which is basically what helps to finance the core of Intel’s operation. The segment was up 5% Y/Y, demonstrating how the cycle is turning in favor of Intel thanks to the PC refresh to Windows 11. I have to say that I’ve rarely seen such a company turn from a laggard with everything against it to all positives. Though Intel’s foundry is currently operating at a loss, which I expected, it is narrowing, and again this is a long-term play, a bet on American manufacturing that has been secured by all the funding that Intel has recently received.

Looking ahead, Intel is being cautiously optimistic, which is generally a good sign for shareholders, as its Q4 guidance is perceived to be conservative. Intel is expecting revenue to come in the range of $12.8B and $13.8, while adjusted non-GAAP EPS is set to come in at 8 cents a share. Now it is important to note that the guidance excludes revenue from Altera, which Intel just sold a majority stake in. This should remove up to $500MM in revenue from the Q4 forecast.

On the broader industry outlook, there has been another tailwind for Intel-based products with management noting that the current demand is outpacing supply for CPUs and that should persist into 2026. This means the market is currently facing supply shortages, which is particularly good for Intel as its 10nm and 7nm manufacturing processes are in high demand.

I also want to touch on what I believe is probably the most crucial dynamic for Intel right now, technology-wise. Intel’s 18A could become a fundamental shift in chip architecture and has become the cornerstone of the company’s turnaround plan to go back to its roots as the leader in chip innovation. I haven’t delved that much into the process in my previous analyses, and I believe that it is time to shed light on it. 18A stands for 1.8-nanometer class manufacturing process, which should help Intel catch up to Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) and Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd. (OTCPK:SSNLF) manufacturing-wise and even leapfrog them if successful. 18A is set to combine two brand-new techs, PowerVia and RibbonFET, to outpace TSMC’s 2nm node, which does not have backside power delivery. And though this is a massive endeavor, Intel is currently on track in its timeline as 18A has entered production at its new Fab 52 in Chandler, Arizona, with products such as the CPUs Panther Lake for AI PCs that will be widely available starting in January 2026, as well as the new Xeon 6+. Another 18A win, as well as a foundry win, would be the recent announcement that Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) will be using the 18A process to manufacture its next generation of custom AI accelerator, boosting the narrative around Intel gaining back market share.

Overall, I believe that this was a great quarter for Intel, not only for its double beat but more so for the implementation of Lip-Bu Tan’s strategy that is currently massively paying off for shareholders.

Valuation

As the company is currently undergoing its transition towards profitability and strategic partnerships, the current valuation might be slightly skewed by the prior poor results. Though the recent net income figure was largely driven by the sale of stakes in Altera as well as Mobileye Global Inc. (MBLY), which made Intel receive around $5B in cash from these transactions. Intel remains somewhat expensive with its current financials, yet there’s way more optimism now for the company than ever in the company’s recent history. Its current EV/EBITDA of 22.7 is high, though it is certainly well below Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) and NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) and far higher than TSMC.

It is important to note that the current valuation hinges on the turnaround potential of Intel and, therefore, there is heightened risk in investing in Intel now. Albeit, it is fair to say that the company has one of the biggest advantages available on the market going for it, the government’s backing.

On the growth front, I expressed a lot of optimism about 18A, but Intel will need to be able to convince customers to buy the next generation of chipsets. The company is grappling with a higher selling mix of older products, which is strong for overall profitability, but it leaves us wondering if current customers are enticed by what Intel can offer on the technological front. Especially now that AMD has captured that end of the market with its Ryzen chipsets, which will probably see the same kind of macro tailwinds as Intel with the PC growth and demand.

For profitability, I mentioned how much I was looking at the Client Computing Group, and I was pleased to see that Intel reported a strong operating income of $2.7B on the back of the heightened demand, far higher than the previous quarters. The same trend can be seen for Data Center and AI, which have reached an EBIT of $964MM, the highest since Q1 2022, probably on the back of their struck partnerships with companies like Nvidia.

Still, Intel has a long road ahead to regain the levels of its peers, such as AMD. The foundry business continues to be a loss-making enterprise, but I do not take it against Intel for the time being, as it is what has pushed for the recent investments in the company to make it a viable contender against TSMC. It will require a lot of CapEx ahead but should become a viable option at the technological front for many fabless semiconductors.

You may notice that my valuation segment differs from my usual analysis, but it is mostly because Intel is currently one of those rare companies that is undergoing such a drastic shift in its overall investment thesis that we should look at it outside of simple value-based investing eyes.

Risks

The main risk for Intel right now would be if the current turnaround of the company does not work out long-term, as it is being aggressive in its cost-cutting endeavors, potentially slowing down innovation for the company. It is still facing high challenges despite the recent tailwinds, and a set of a few weak quarters could erode investors’ confidence.

Intel's strategy also heavily relies on the 18A process node and the ability to mass-produce it. If the company falls behind on its strategy, even if it stated that it was on track, this could directly be a major setback as customers are now banking on Intel going back to the top of the technological edge. Furthermore, being at the front of the foundry process could be the main and only selling point for customers to use the fab, as Intel is still a competitor for many of them, while TSMC is not.

Conclusion

With all of that in mind, strategic investments and quarterly earnings, I believe that currently investing in Intel could be interesting for risk-profile investors considering all the momentum that the company has garnered in a relatively short time period. There is a lot of hype and possibilities for everything to go wrong, yet Intel is currently in possibly the most interesting period of its history. I rate Intel as a very small Buy.
